Standard dialogue tags said asked. mind skips over them, they invisible.
hotly debated whether should ever colorful adverb tags," muttered. "Some editors don't mind creative tags."
"Some allow adverb tags," said skeptically. "Adverb tags generally frowned upon."
Break this rule your peril," said mischievously.
Your dialogue should look like these examples. Note correct formatting. Commas, periods, question marks should fall within quotation marks.
comma separates dialogue from standard tag. period separates dialogue from action tag.
same formatting standard applies creative action tags.
Standard front: Sherlock said, see."
Standard middle: see," Sherlock said. have been misinformed."
Standard end: see," Sherlock said. (or) see," said Sherlock.
Action front: Sherlock cleared throat. see."
Action middle: see." Sherlock cleared throat. misinformed."
Action end: see.
Dialogue, just talk?
This great topic, Diana, that trips writers. reminds some manuscripts I've edited where page filled with short exchanges between characters, writer identifies each conversation with said" "she said". Overuse dialogue tags--whether standard power scene, while proper enhances writers need remember that.
Great reminders that "said" "asked" invisible reader. only other like (sparingly) "whispered."
Good post. don't like adverb tags, either. content your writing should tell your acting mischievously skeptically. these tags older books.
don't mind occasional adverb tag, long it's over used. Great post.
long trip, listened bestselling author's book, filled with adverb tags. Hearing them throughout more than annoying. that reason, never them. adhere Elmore Leonard's rules, you'd never them all. disagree. Adverbs have their place, just dialogue tag. rule whatever works sentence what humbug rules.
